content: A significant counter-argument to aggressive fiscal stimulus is the crowding-out effect. When governments borrow heavily to fund deficit spending, they compete with private borrowers for available capital, potentially driving up interest rates and reducing private investment. Additionally, persistent large deficits increase government debt-to-GDP ratios, which can lead to higher future taxes or inflation. Research by Reinhart and Rogoff suggests that debt levels above 90% of GDP correlate with slower economic growth, though this threshold is debated. The timing of fiscal policy also matters—implementation lags mean stimulus may arrive after the economy has already begun recovering.
